intro:
The arm of the LORD (Hebrew zĕrôaʿ YHWH) is a vivid anthropomorphism for God's active, saving power — his strength stretched out to deliver, to judge, and to gather his people. As a man's arm is the limb by which he works and fights, so "the arm of the LORD" names God himself at work in the world with irresistible might. It is one of Scripture's richest images of redemption, and it culminates in a startling revelation: the arm of the LORD takes on flesh in Jesus Christ.

The phrase is forged in the exodus. God promises, "I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with great acts of judgment" (Exodus 6:6), and Israel is taught ever after to remember that the LORD brought them out "with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm" (Deuteronomy 4:34; Deuteronomy 5:15). The arm is the power that no Pharaoh can resist. In worship Israel sings, "his right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation for him" (Psalm 98:1), confessing that deliverance comes from God's strength alone, not human help.

The prophets long for that arm to act again. In exile the people cry, "Awake, awake, put on strength, O arm of the LORD… as in days of old" (Isaiah 51:9), pleading for a new exodus. God answers that he will do it openly: "The LORD has bared his holy arm before the eyes of all the nations, and all the ends of the earth shall see the salvation of our God" (Isaiah 52:10). The saving power once shown at the sea will be displayed before the whole world.

Then comes the great surprise. The very next song asks, "Who has believed what he has heard? And to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed?" — and the answer is the Suffering Servant, who grew up "like a young plant," despised and rejected, pierced for our transgressions (Isaiah 53:1–2; Isaiah 53:5). The arm of the LORD — God's mighty, world-saving power — is unveiled not in a conquering army but in a crucified Servant. This is the gospel Paul proclaims: Christ crucified is "the power of God and the wisdom of God," strength made perfect in apparent weakness (1 Corinthians 1:23–24). Mary had already sung that God "has shown strength with his arm" in the coming of her Son (Luke 1:51).

The arm of the LORD matters because it tells us where to look for the saving power of God. It is not finally a metaphor but a person: Jesus Christ, in whom God bared his holy arm before the nations and accomplished a greater exodus from sin and death. To trust the arm of the LORD is to trust the Servant in whom that arm was revealed — and to know that the strength which split the sea now works salvation through the cross.
